data.title: London Stock Exchange Group's Growth Prospects Look PositiveMarket Talk
data.description: London Stock Exchange Group's growth profile looks attractive, Deutsche Bank's Benjamin Goy writes in a note. The stock-exchange and financial-information company's revenues are largely recurring, the analyst says. The company has some pricing power, supported by an improving data & analytics product. Additionally, transaction-based revenues benefit from structural growth drivers, amplified by current market volatility, he adds. Therefore, the German bank expects more than 7% revenue growth going forward. DB upgrades its stock recommendation to buy from hold and its target price to 12,600 pence from 11,500 pence. Shares are up 1.6% at 11,255 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

data.title: Inception Growth Acquisition Limited Announces Additional Contribution to Trust Account to Extend Business Combination Period
data.description:
New York, April 15, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Inception Growth Acquisition Limited (NASDAQ: IGTA, the “Company”), a publicly traded special purpose acquisition company, announced today that on April 10, 2025, the Company deposited $11,199.60 into the Company’s trust account (the “Trust Account”)  in order to extend the period of time the Company has to complete a business combination for an additional one (1) month period, from April 13, 2025 to May 13, 2025. The purpose of the extension is to provide additional time for the Company to complete a business combination.

About Inception Growth Acquisition Limited

Inception Growth Acquisition Limited is a blank check company incorporated under the laws of Delaware whose business purpose is to effect a merger, capital stock exchange, asset acquisition, stock purchase, reorganization, or similar business combination with one or more businesses or entities. 

Forward Looking Statements

This press release includes forward-looking statements that involve risks and uncertainties. Forward-looking statements are statements that are not historical facts. Such forward-looking statements, including the successful consummation of the Company’s initial public offering, are subject to risks and uncertainties, which could cause actual results to differ from the forward-looking statements. The Company expressly disclaims any obligations or undertaking to release publicly any updates or revisions to any forward-looking statements contained herein to reflect any change in the Company’s expectations with respect thereto or any change in events, conditions or circumstances on which any statement is based.

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1015 GMT - London Stock Exchange Group's growth profile looks attractive, Deutsche Bank's Benjamin Goy writes in a note. The stock-exchange and financial-information company's revenues are largely recurring, the analyst says. The company has some pricing power, supported by an improving data & analytics product. Additionally, transaction-based revenues benefit from structural growth drivers, amplified by current market volatility, he adds. Therefore, the German bank expects more than 7% revenue growth going forward. DB upgrades its stock recommendation to buy from hold and its target price to 12,600 pence from 11,500 pence. Shares are up 1.6% at 11,255 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

1011 GMT - LVMH's results will likely set a negative tone for the 1Q reporting season, Citi analysts Thomas Chauvet and Mahesh Mohankumar write in a note. The performance of the French luxury group, considered a sector bellwether, was overall below the most conservative buy-side expectations, Citi says. With highly elevated economic uncertainty, both in the key U.S. market and globally, it is difficult to build a credible scenario of sequential revenue improvement in the coming two quarters for LVMH and the luxury sector, the analysts say. Consensus expectations for full-year sales and EBIT could be reduced by a low-single-digit and high-single-digit percentage, respectively, they say. Shares trade 7.5% lower at 490.50 euros. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0955 GMT - Publicis delivered a solid first-quarter update that should limit cuts to consensus estimates driven by economic concerns at least for this year, UBS analysts say in a research note. The French advertising group said net revenue grew 4.9% organically in the first quarter, topping expectations of 4.5%. It still expects organic growth of 4% to 5% for the year, with a second-quarter performance within that range. Investors should react positively to the reaffirmation of the 2025 guidance and the new outlook for the second quarter, the analysts say. UBS expects Publicis' second-quarter organic growth to be 4.5%. Shares rise 3.1% to 86.60 euros. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0955 GMT - A U.S. investigation into pharmaceutical imports and potential tariffs in the coming months will likely limit clarity from pharma companies during their 1Q results, Barclays analysts say in a note. The investigation, initiated April 1, aims to assess the national security implications of pharmaceutical imports, including finished generics, nongenerics and active pharmaceutical ingredients, and has to be finalized in around nine months. "With Pharma having been excluded from the initial reciprocal tariffs and it being our house view that some sort of Commerce Department investigation being required for these sectoral tariffs, this isn't too surprising but at least provides some timing framing," they say. Barclays analysts estimate that AstraZeneca, Roche and Grifols appear most insulated, whereas ​GSK and Sandoz look most exposed to a potential tariff risk. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0952 GMT - LVMH's sales miss confirms a tough start to the year, Jefferies analysts write in a note. The luxury conglomerate of French billionaire Bernard Arnault logged sales of 20.31 billion euros for the first quarter, missing Visible Alpha consensus of 21.21 billion euros. Analysts had forecast a challenging start to 2025 due to softening trends in the U.S. after the pickup in the prior quarter. To mitigate the potential hit from U.S. tariffs, LVMH could consider price hikes and an increase in production in its facilities in Texas. Looking ahead, the uncertainty around consumers' willingness to spend at the higher price points in the U.S. and Europe as well as the extent to which Chinese consumers will rebound, will be critical, Jefferies says. Shares fall 7%. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0952 GMT - The market is now better prepared for any potential ADR delistings, Julius Baer analysts write in an email. While the market generally assumes that trade tensions between the U.S. and China have peaked, there is still a possibility of Chinese American Depository Receipts being delisted, they add. More Chinese companies have returned to Hong Kong for secondary or dual primary listing compared with that four years ago, they say. Converting ADRs to Hong Kong shares remains a sensible option for investors looking to mitigate the risk of potential delistings, they say. The analysts continue to expect consolidation or correction pressure throughout the second quarter. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0950 GMT - Halfords' business changes are underway, and the company is yielding strong benefits from its strategy, AJ Bell investment director Russ Mould says in a note. The U.K. motoring-and-cycling products provider's integrated service, Fusion, creates cohesion between its retail outlets and its car repair garages. These links are boosting revenue and increasing business resilience given that most people need to keep their cars working, he says. The Fusion segment also provides a solid platform for incoming CEO Henry Birch, who is replacing Graham Stapleton after a seven-year stint, Mould adds. Additionally, the company has found ways to mitigate the impact of higher costs associated with the U.K.'s autumn budget, he says. Shares are up more than 12%. (michael.susin@wsj.com)

0948 GMT - Publicis' 2025 results should be unaffected by any potential cuts to client spending against the background of tariff uncertainty, but their impact would materialize next year, CEO Arthur Sadoun says. "Whatever we could lose within the year won't have an impact before next year," Sadoun says in a call with analysts. Similarly, any additional business wins in what is left of 2025 won't have a big impact in this year's results given the time it takes to secure the account and ramp up work, he says. Shares rise 2.9% to 86.48 euros. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0946 GMT - FirstGroup's earnings estimates for FY 2025 get a boost after its performance update reinforces a strong balance sheet, RBC Capital Markets analyst Ruairi Cullinane says in a note. The transport operator's earnings per share expectations are lifted around 3% to 18.6 pence on higher anticipated variable fees in the rail division, Cullinane says. "FirstGroup offers attractions including relatively defensive credentials within the transport sector, and both a strong balance sheet and recent track record of delivery," he says. Shares are up 1.8% at 162.20 pence. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0936 GMT - Publicis Chairman and CEO Arthur Sadoun says he isn't worried about revenue falling off a cliff due to tariff uncertainty, citing new business wins that can make up for unforeseen cuts in client spending. "New business is what makes us very confident to offset potential cuts that we could not have seen at the beginning of the year," Sadoun says in a call with analysts. In a first-quarter update, the French advertising group confirmed its outlook for 2025, which calls for organic net revenue growth of 4% to 5%. Shares rise 3% to 86.52 euros. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0932 GMT - LVMH's first-quarter figures provide a negative readacross for its peers in the luxury space, Deutsche Bank analysts write in a note. The French conglomerate booked sales of 20.31 billion euros for the period, 3% down organically compared with the prior year and missing analysts' forecasts. The group noted that the main drag was Chinese demand in Japan. The lack of a dramatic slowdown in LVMH sales in the U.S. in March is somewhat reassuring given all of the noise, the analysts say. "But we suspect this may be yet to be fully reflected in the data given weaker consumer confidence," they add. Shares trade 6.9% lower at 493.75 euros. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0929 GMT - London Stock Exchange Group shouldn't be directly affected by U.S. tariffs, JPMorgan Cazenove analysts write in a research note. While the company has a large exposure to the U.S. dollar, the majority of its revenue are recurring in nature. "A large proportion of its volume-dependent revenues can benefit from an increase in market volatility," they say. Still, LSEG has significantly underperformed other stock markets since the start of the year, partly due to perceived exposure to the U.S. market following tariff announcements, they add. Shares are up 1.2% at 112.10 pounds.(najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

data.title: Australian Central Bankers May Adjust Policy in May: April Minutes
data.description:
The Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) may cut interest rates at its pending May meeting, reveal minutes from the central bank's March 31-April 1 policy meeting, released Tuesday.

At the early April meeting, the RBA held its key policy interest rate steady at 4.10%, after having reduced the rate by 0.25% in its mid-February policy session.

But at the RBA April meeting, central bankers thought the pending May meeting, slated for May 19-20, may afford an opportunity to adjust rates.

"Members observed that the May meeting would be an opportune time to revisit the monetary policy setting with the benefit of additional data about inflation, wages, the labor market and trends in economic activity," recorded the RBA minutes.

By mid-May, there would be a fresh set of economic forecasts and further information about the likely evolution of global trade policies, added RBA members.

The RBA has a target rate of inflation band of 2% to 3%, on the nation's consumer price index (CPI).

Of late, Australia's inflation rate has cooled. The nation's CPI rose 2.4% on year in February, easing from a 2.5% on-year gain in January.

Excluding volatile items and travel, the CPI rose 2.7% on year in the month, slowing from a 2.9% gain in January.

Nevertheless, RBA board members in April did not want to signal certainty regarding a May interest-rate cut.

Members "agreed that it would be helpful if the (RBA) Board's public communication following the (April) meeting made it clear that the outcome of its next decision was not predetermined," according to meeting minutes.

data.title: US Goverment Probes Impacts of Pharmaceutical, Semiconductor Imports on National Security
data.description:
The US government is looking into the impacts of pharmaceutical and semiconductor imports on national security before implementing tariffs on these sectors, with the Department of Commerce launching probes April 1, according to documents filed Monday.

The Department of Commerce also launched 21-day public comment periods for both sectors, the documents showed.

According to the filings, the probe covers imports of pharmaceuticals, pharmaceutical ingredients, and their derivative products, and semiconductors, semiconductor manufacturing equipment, and their derivative products.

data.title: UK stocks rise after Trump hints at possible auto tariff reprieve
data.description:
Key points:
  • FTSE 100 up 0.9%, FTSE 250 rises 0.5%
  • Aston Martin and Dowlais Group rise on possible tariff reprieve
  • Investment banking firms leads gains
  • Data showed employers cut jobs in run-up to tax hike

UK's main indexes rose on Tuesday with financial shares leading the broad-based gains after U.S. President Donald Trump signalled possible exemptions on auto-related levies, while investors also assessed job market data for monetary policy insights amid global economic uncertainty.

As of 0958 GMT, the blue-chip FTSE 100 index CURRENCYCOM:UK100 was up 0.9% and the midcap index gained 0.5%.

Trump indicated he was considering adjusting the 25% tariffs on foreign auto and auto parts imports from Mexico, Canada and other regions. This followed Friday's exemption of smartphones, computers and other electronics from his "reciprocal" tariffs.

After recent market selloffs, investors seized on the positive news, pushing shares higher for the second consecutive session.

Luxury carmaker Aston Martin and automotive parts supplier Dowlais Group advanced 4% and 2%, respectively.

Investment banking and brokerage service index (.FTNMX302020) led gains, rising 3%, with investment firm 3I group topping the FTSE 100 with a 4% increase after Citigroup raised its target price on the stock.

Precious metal miners also strengthened as gold prices climbed, with investors turning to the safe-haven asset amid ongoing uncertainty about U.S. tariff plans. Fresnillo , Hochschild and Endeavour Mining gained between 4% to 2%.

Aerospace and Defence index (.FTNMX502010) climbed 2%, with Rolls Royce rising 2.4% after the Pentagon named Rolls-Royce Solutions America as the principal contractor for a potential sale of Eitan PowerPack engines to Israel.

Frasers was among the top performers on the midcap index, up 3.3%, after the sportswear and fashion group announced an agreement with Australian footwear retailer Accent Group to launch and operate Sports Direct across Australia and New Zealand.

On the data front, Britain's labour market weakened ahead of this month's tax hike on employers, but wage growth remained strong, complicating the job for the Bank of England, which is also expecting an economic hit from U.S. trade tariffs.

Investors were pricing a more than 90% probability on the BoE cutting rates by a quarter of a percentage point on May 8.

data.title: ING Comments on Euro, Sterling, Czech Republic's Koruna
data.description:
The German ZEW surveys published Tuesday are the first activity indicators to be released in the eurozone since the "liberation day" announcement of United States President Donald Trump, noted ING.

Respondents are financial market experts, not business managers like the Ifo. Consensus is for a big drop in the expectation gauge from March's 52 to 10 on the back of U.S. tariffs, wrote the bank in a note to clients.

However, some responses may have been collected before the tariff pause announced last Wednesday by Trump. Markets may not dwell too much on a soft figure, stated ING.

is overbought and overvalued, but the bank pointed out it observed strong buying interest around 1.130, and it still thinks the near-term bias is towards 1.15 rather than 1.12 from the current 1.135.

After all, the US dollar (USD) slump remains largely a function of the loss of the USD's appeal as a reserve/safe-haven asset, and the euro's high liquidity character should continue to absorb a lot of the rotation. The major risk at this stage is perhaps very dovish signals by the European Central Bank as it cuts on Thursday, although markets are pricing in 75bps of total easing this year, so the bar is set relatively high, added ING.

Elsewhere in Europe, the United Kingdom released jobs figures for March earlier Tuesday. Payrolls fell more than expected, extending 2024's modest private-sector downtrend, though these numbers often see upward revisions. Despite survey warnings, there's little hard evidence yet of the employer tax hike triggering layoffs, noted the bank.

Wage growth came in softer than consensus, but underlying private-sector pay dynamics remain firm. With unemployment data still unreliable, payrolls are the key metric to watch. Bottom line: nothing here to shift the Bank of England's stance.

ING sticks to its call for a May rate cut, followed by quarterly cuts into 2026.

With ECB and BoE upcoming cuts — Thursday and in May — fully in the price, remains almost solely a function of risk sentiment. EUR's safe-haven appeal means further selloffs will take back higher. Equity futures point to more tentative stabilization, meaning the pair can continue hedging lower towards 0.850, also helped by the ECB's moving first on easing.

Monday trading indicated a lack of interest in Central and Eastern European (CEE) markets these days and the next move will again be determined by global events, according to ING. Markets are likely to remain in this muted mode this week and next.

However, later on, investors will get closer to the central bank meetings in late April and early May, which should again attract market attention. Rates space is becoming more immune to spikes in the core markets, and at the same time, it's hard to be received in most places in CEE, given the current dovish market pricing.

At the same time, the equity market rally hasn't translated into the usual rally in CEE currencies either on Monday. It's almost as if the CEE markets didn't want to be dragged into this "global mess." Given the lack of conviction in the direction of the markets, the bank prefers relative value within CEE and maintains a stronger Czech Republic Koruna (CZK) view versus the rest of the CEE market.

data.title: India stocks, rupee, swaps, call at 3:30 p.m. IST
data.description:
STOCKS:

The benchmark BSE Sensex was up 2.1% at 76,734.89, while the broader NSE index advanced 2.2% to 23,328.55, joining a global relief rally on Tuesday after U.S. President Donald Trump signalled more tariff exemptions.

RUPEE:

The Indian rupee rose 0.3% versus the U.S. dollar to 85.7725, aided by likely dollar inflows into local stocks, although traders said volatility is likely to persist until there is clarity on U.S. tariffs.

GOVERNMENT BONDS:

The benchmark 10-year bond (IN067934G=CC) was quoted at 102.655 rupees, with the yield down 3 basis points at 6.4114%, as the central bank's continued liquidity support aided investor sentiment.

OVERNIGHT INDEX SWAPS:

The one-year overnight index swap rate (INR1YMIBROIS=CC) was down 4 bps at 5.74%, while the five-year swap rate (INR5YMIBROIS=CC) dropped 2 bps to 5.6925%.

CALL MONEY:

India's overnight call money rate (INROND=) was at 5.40% and the overnight TREPS rate (INTREPTOT=TCCL) was at 5.77%.

data.title: Kremlin says there is no clear outline for U.S.-Russia deal on Ukraine, but there is political will
data.description:
The Kremlin said on Tuesday that there is no clear outline of a U.S.-Russia deal on Ukraine for now, but that there is political will to move in the direction of an agreement.

In a call with reporters, Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said that contacts with the U.S. were positive and useful, but declined to give a timeline for a peace settlement.

Ukraine and some of its European allies say Russia is not serious about wanting to end the war and have accused it of dragging out the talks with the U.S.

data.title: Inflows lift rupee, but traders reckon tariff volatility may persist
data.description:
The Indian rupee rose on Tuesday, diverging from most Asian peers, aided by likely dollar inflows into local stocks, although traders said volatility is likely to persist until there is clarity on U.S. tariffs.

The rupee closed at 85.7725 against the dollar, up 0.3%. The currency touched a peak of 85.59 before trimming gains in the face of importer hedging demand.

India's benchmark equity indexes, BSE Sensex and Nifty 50 , ended higher by over 2% each.

The indexes have erased all of their losses since the announcement of U.S. tariffs on April 2, outperforming their regional peers.

There was a "lot of custodial (dollar) selling," which helped the rupee out, a salesperson at a foreign bank said. Broad-based dollar offers also picked up on the back of the likely inflows, the salesperson said.

Other Asian currencies were mostly lower, while the dollar index continued to hover near its lowest levels since April 2022 with constant shifts in U.S. trade policies denting its appeal.

While smartphones and other electronics have been exempted from "reciprocal tariffs," comments from U.S. President Donald Trump suggested that the reprieve is likely to be short-lived.

"Markets retain a substantial risk premium attached to U.S. assets, including the dollar," ING Bank said in a note.

The greenback has declined 8% this year, battered by concerns of a slowdown in the U.S. economy and as investors pulled out from U.S. assets following the announcement of sweeping tariffs.

While a broadly weaker dollar has aided emerging market currencies, traders reckon that volatility is set to persist amid near-constant shifts in U.S. trade policy.

The dollar-rupee's 1-month realised volatility and the 1-month implied volatility are both perched near two-year highs.

Meanwhile, India's merchandise trade deficit (INTRD=ECI) in March stood at $21.54 billion, higher than the $16 billion anticipated by economists in a Reuters poll.

data.title: US Stocks Brief: S&P Futures Up 0.15% After US President Trump Talks of Potential Auto Tariffs Pause
The 10-Point: The Wall Street Journal's Guide to the Day's Top News
data.description:
By Emma Tucker

A little-known government task force formed to combat antisemitism has shaken elite American universities to their core in recent weeks. It has targeted billions of dollars in federal funding at premier institutions such as Columbia and Harvard, with cascading effects on campuses nationwide. Separately, President Trump is touting Nvidia's plan to produce made-in-America AI supercomputers as a victory in his trade war. But just how much of Nvidia's plan was really new?

Today's Headlines

The Trump administration froze $2.26 billion in funding to Harvard hours after the university refused to agree to its demands.

The White House gave automakers a tariff break for American-made parts. Executives are puzzled over how to collect it.

Trump's escalating tariff spat with Beijing threatens to push Chinese consumers even further into the arms of domestic brands.

Yemeni militias are planning a ground offensive against the Houthis after a U.S. bombing campaign degraded the militant group's capabilities.

Israel has taken over about a third of the Gaza Strip, declaring security zones while pushing out Palestinians from those areas.

Live From The Markets

Banks' trading desks are minting money from Trump's tariff chaos.

Netflix and Spotify are better positioned for a recession than their rivals, but resilience isn't immunity, writes Dan Gallagher.

Read It Here First

A government task force is tearing through American universities.

Called the Task Force to Combat Anti-Semitism, the group's stated goal is to "root out antisemitic harassment in schools and on college campuses," a mission that emerged after pro-Palestinian protests last year. But along the way, the task force is taking on university culture more broadly in ways that echo the MAGA dreams for remaking higher education — including ending racial preferences in admissions and hiring, Liz Essley Whyte, Douglas Belkin and Sara Randazzo report.

Nvidia's made-in-the-U.S.A. move follows a familiar Trump pattern.

In 2019, President Trump and Apple CEO Tim Cook toured a facility in Austin where workers assembled Mac Pro computers. The site had been in operation since 2013. Trump touted it at the time as "the beginning of a very powerful, important plant." Now, Nvidia is joining the ranks of U.S. tech companies seeking to meet Trump's demands on manufacturing. Not all aspects of its plan are new. And as with similar announcements during Trump's first administration, Nvidia hasn't said what volume of products or capacity it's ultimately targeting, Bradley Olson and Rolfe Winkler report.

Expert Take

Q: What are banks saying about business activity as Trump's trade war unfolds?

Many of America's biggest national and regional banks shared their first-quarter results over the past few days, offering clues on what businesses are doing with their money. So far, looming tariffs aren't forcing companies to reengineer their businesses — they're freezing them, Telis Demos reports.

A: Wells Fargo and JPMorgan Chase last week both said they didn't see indicators that companies were scrambling to do big drawdowns on credit lines to raise cash. And on Monday, Buffalo-based M&T Bank reported that the utilization rate — how much of a credit line a customer is using — was actually down in the first quarter among its commercial clients.

For banks, these drawdowns are a double-edged sword. On the one hand, they add to loan growth and demonstrates banks' ability to be in their customers' corner during a panic. But they also rapidly add to bank assets at a time when they might otherwise choose to be cautious about who they lend to.

M&T finance chief Daryl Bible said that, overall, "our customers actually really wanted to make a lot of investments. They want to do acquisitions." However, "they are just really on pause right now...They don't know what the rules of the road are right now," he added.

Average commercial-and-industrial loans at U.S. banks grew just 0.9% last quarter from the year prior, according to Federal Reserve data. Outside of the wild swings in those numbers since Covid-19, that would've been the slowest pace from 2012 through 2019.

See The Story

Kristi Noem is taking a made-for-TV approach to Homeland Security.

Just days after Noem took office as the new head of the agency, she accompanied ICE officials on a predawn raid in New York City — and posted about it on X. The problem: The raid was still under way when her post went up. Noem's approach to her role often places her at center stage as the face of the president's push to get tough on illegal border crossers, deport millions and scare more migrants from coming to America. Inside the department, some view her as more of a chief spokeswoman than a traditional secretary closely guiding the agency's direction.

Happening Today

The deadline to file U.S. taxes. (Need an extension? Follow a few simple steps.)

Harvey Weinstein will face a new trial on sexual-assault charges in New York.

Earnings: Johnson & Johnson, Bank of America, Citigroup, PNC, United Airlines, JB Hunt Transport Services, Albertsons

The number of rental units the new-construction pipeline delivered in 2023 and 2024, according to property-data firm Yardi Matrix, the most since the mid-1980s. With the cost of imported materials poised to soar and construction labor getting harder to find, this crush of overbuilding suddenly looks like a lucky break for the apartment industry.

And Finally...

Meet the parents who accompany their high-school seniors on spring break.

High-school seniors may be leaving home for college in a few months, but some parents aren't ready to let them party on their own in countries where the drinking age is 18.

data.title: Top of the Street: Barry Callebaut, JD Sports, LVMH, Pandora, Partners Group
data.description:
A round-up of notable broker activity this morning from Europe's top-ranked* analysts:

** Barclays cuts Barry Callebaut to "equal-weight" from "overweight", saying the risk/reward looks much less appealing considering cocoa prices, risk of high fixed costs due to the trade war and weaker pricing power vs guidance

** Barclays raises JD Sports Fashion to "equal-weight" from "underweight" citing improved capital allocation and better discounted stock-specific risks

** Morgan Stanley cuts LVMH to "equal weight" from "overweight" on the softer than expected start to the year, with all its divisions missing top line expectations

** Jyske Bank raises Pandora to "buy" from "hold", saying the stock price has started to look "interesting" again after a recent drop put it at a 16% discount vs historical average

** Citigroup cuts Partners Group Holding to "neutral" from "buy" as the private equity firm seems to be exposed to fundraising and currency risks

** Exane BNP Paribas cuts Nestle to "neutral" from outperform" on valuation grounds after strong share price performance, coupled with the broker's earnings revision

** Kepler Cheuvreux raises Wartsila to "buy" from "hold", saying the Finnish engineering group's shares should be fairly unaffected by the current trade turmoil

(*Analyst rankings from Thomson Reuters StarMine. The scale is from 1-star to 5-star with 5 being the best. Analysts are ranked on earnings accuracy as well as relative performance of recommendations over trailing 12-month & 24-month periods.)

data.title: Binance among crypto exchanges hit by Amazon Web Services outage
data.description:
Crypto exchange Binance said it briefly stopped allowing users to withdraw from the platform on Tuesday, due to an issue involving an Amazon Web Services (AWS) data centre, while other crypto exchanges also reported problems.

Binance posted on X that withdrawals were suspended "to keep safe", before posting around ten minutes later that withdrawals had resumed. A spokesperson for Binance said withdrawals were suspended for around 23 minutes.

AWS's service dashboard showed a "connectivity issue" affected its Tokyo cloud region at 1.15 a.m. PDT (0815 GMT). The issue was resolved by 1.51 a.m. PDT (0851 GMT).

“The issue has been resolved and the service is operating normally,” an AWS spokesperson said.

Another crypto exchange, KuCoin , also said it was experiencing "temporary disruptions".

"Our technical team is urgently working on a fix, and the recovery time will be announced separately. Rest assured that your assets remain secure and all data is intact," KuCoin said in a post on X.

A spokesperson for KuCoin said via email that the disruption was due to an AWS outage that affected services across the Tokyo data centre.

"Some services have already been restored, and our team is working closely with AWS to recover full functionality as quickly as possible. No user assets or data have been affected," the spokesperson said.

Crypto exchange MEXC said in a post on X that due to AWS disruption, users may experience issues including "abnormal candlestick charts", "failed order cancellations" and "delays in asset transfers for spot trading".

"We want to assure you that your assets on MEXC remain fully secure. For any losses incurred as a result of this platform-related issue, we will prepare a compensation plan to appropriately reimburse affected users," MEXC said.
